does: This paper investigates how large language models internally represent and organize behavioral traits during pretraining by tracing persona vectors—linear directions in neural activations corresponding to behaviors like sycophancy or evilness—across the full training run of OLMo-3-7B. The authors find that these semantic directions emerge extremely early in pretraining, within just 0.22% of total training steps, and remain functionally stable for steering behavior in downstream instruct-tuned models. They demonstrate that while core persona vector representations solidify early, these directions continue to undergo geometric and semantic refinement throughout the remaining pretraining. The work also compares multiple elicitation strategies for extracting persona vectors and shows that different prompting approaches yield effective but qualitatively distinct directional representations, suggesting multiple valid geometric encodings of the same behavioral concepts.
connects: This work extends recent advances in linear representational interpretability which demonstrated that high-level model behaviors map to linear directions in activation space, building on techniques for detecting and intervening on safety-relevant traits. It directly responds to a gap in mechanistic understanding by applying the established persona vector framework diachronically—asking not just whether such vectors exist in trained models, but how and when they form during training. The paper connects to broader lines of work on representation learning in neural networks and mechanistic interpretability, particularly efforts to understand when semantic structure emerges in language models. It also relates to ongoing research on behavioral steering and audit methods in LLMs, providing empirical grounding for why these interventions work despite post-training modifications.
enables: This work enables several downstream research directions in interpretability and safety. Understanding that persona vectors form early and persist suggests they may represent fundamental structural aspects of how models encode behavioral dispositions, opening questions about what determines the emergence of specific geometric directions. The finding that multiple elicitation strategies surface different facets of behavior enables more sophisticated auditing frameworks that characterize behavioral complexity through multiple representational lenses. Early emergence of these vectors suggests potential intervention points earlier in training pipelines and raises questions about their initialization from pretraining data. The results also motivate investigations into whether controlling or shaping persona vectors during training could enforce safety properties at the representational level, potentially improving robustness beyond post-hoc steering.
